county_metadata.parent_county: Territory of Hawaii (county system created by Act 39, Session Laws 1905)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hawaii County was formed in 1905 under Act 39 of the Territorial Legislature, encompassing the entire island of Hawaii (the Big Island), the largest island in the Hawaiian chain. County seat is Hilo. The Big Island includes the districts of Hilo, Hamakua, North Kohala, South Kohala, North Kona, South Kona, Ka'u, and Puna -- these historical districts predate the county system and are used in land records and Hawaiian genealogical research. The island has active volcanism (Kilauea, Mauna Loa) that has shaped settlement patterns and occasionally affected communities and records. Major sugar and ranching operations (Parker Ranch in Waimea is one of the oldest and largest cattle ranches in the US) shaped the county's demographics.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hawaii County covers the entire Big Island, the largest island in the chain, encompassing diverse communities from Hilo on the windward side to Kona on the leeward coast. The Hawaii County Bureau of Conveyances office in Hilo can provide local assistance, but all land records are recorded centrally at the Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu -- search the 'Regular System' for post-1845 land transactions and the 'Land Court System' for registered land. For Hawaiian families, the Great Mahele awards (1848-1855) for Hawaii Island are in the Mahele Book and Land Commission Award records at the Hawaii State Archives. Sugar plantation records for Hamakua Coast, Hilo, and Kohala operations are essential for tracing Japanese, Portuguese, Filipino, and Chinese immigrant families -- check the Hawaii Plantation Village archives and the Hawaiian Sugar Planters' Association records at the University of Hawaii at Manoa. The Lyman House Memorial Museum in Hilo holds missionary family papers and early Hilo community records. Volcanic eruptions (especially 1868, 1946 tsunami in Hilo, and recurring Kilauea activity) have affected some local records and communities. The Kona Historical Society holds records for the west Hawaii coast. Buddhist temple records in Hilo and Hamakua are important for Japanese families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu maintains all Big Island land records; Regular System from 1845 and Land Court System from 1903; Great Mahele awards for Hawaii Island districts (Hilo, Kona, Kohala, Hamakua, Ka'u, Puna) are indexed

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaii County Land Records -- Bureau of Conveyances Regular System
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Conveyances, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: All Hawaii County land transactions recorded in the statewide Bureau of Conveyances system; Regular System grantor-grantee index from 1845; includes deeds, mortgages, leases, and agreements for Big Island properties in Hilo, Kona, Kohala, Hamakua, Ka'u, and Puna districts
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival; centralized statewide recording system

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaii County Birth Records -- Hawaii Department of Health
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii Department of Health, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registration of births from 1842 under the Kingdom of Hawaii; Hawaii County births registered through district agents; delayed birth certificates filed for earlier births; Department of Health maintains originals
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very good from Kingdom era; registration compliance improved under Territorial government

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaii County Census Records -- Kingdom and Federal
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Archives / NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii censuses (1866, 1878, 1890) enumerated Hawaii Island residents; US federal census from 1900; 1900 census is especially valuable as the first federal enumeration, listing ethnicity of plantation workers; agricultural schedules document plantation operations

source_profiles.source_name: Big Island Sugar Plantation Employment Records
source_profiles.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa Hamilton Library / Hawaii Plantation Village / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment records from Hamakua Coast, Hilo, Kohala, and Kona sugar plantations documenting contract laborers: Chinese (from 1852), Japanese (from 1868), Portuguese (from 1878), Korean (from 1903), and Filipino (from 1906); includes labor contracts, payroll records, housing assignments, and plantation store ledgers
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by plantation; some collections are extensive while others were lost when plantations closed

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaii County Church and Temple Records -- Protestant Mission, Buddhist, Catholic
source_profiles.repository: Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library / Local congregations / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM) mission station records for Hilo, Kona, Kohala, and Waimea from the 1820s-1830s; Buddhist temple records for Japanese communities in Hilo and Hamakua; Catholic parish records; Hawaiian Evangelical Association records with baptisms, marriages, and deaths of Hawaiian congregants

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaii County Military and WWII Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Hawaii State Archives / Hawaii War Records Depository (UH Manoa)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: WWII-era records for Big Island military installations; Hawaii Territorial Guard and Hawaii National Guard records; WWII internment records for Japanese residents of Hawaii County; draft registration records; veteran discharge records filed with Bureau of Conveyances

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaii County Cemetery Records and Burial Permits
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii County / Find A Grave / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery records for Hilo, Kona, Kohala, and Ka'u cemeteries; burial permits from the Board of Health; Hawaiian church and family burial sites (some on private land); Japanese, Chinese, and Filipino community cemeteries; Homelani Memorial Park (Hilo) and other modern cemeteries

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaii County Circuit Court Records -- Third Circuit
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Judiciary / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Third Circuit Court records for Hawaii County including civil, criminal, probate, and equity cases; Kingdom-era court records; divorce records; guardianship proceedings; naturalization petitions filed in circuit court
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; Kingdom and Territorial court records transferred to Hawaii State Archives

county_metadata.parent_county: Territory of Hawaii (county system created by Act 39, Session Laws 1905)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Honolulu County was formed in 1905 under Act 39 of the Territorial Legislature, encompassing the entire island of Oahu. County seat and state capital is Honolulu, which has served as the seat of Hawaiian government since King Kamehameha III moved the capital from Lahaina in 1845. Honolulu is a consolidated city-county (the City and County of Honolulu). It is by far the most populous county, containing roughly 70% of Hawaii's population. Pearl Harbor Naval Base and extensive military installations have significantly shaped the county's demographics and generated substantial military personnel records. All major state-level repositories are located in Honolulu: Hawaii State Archives, Bureau of Conveyances, Department of Health, Bishop Museum, and the Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library.
county_metadata.research_tips: Honolulu County encompasses the entire island of Oahu and is the center of Hawaiian governance, making it the single most important jurisdiction for genealogical research. The Hawaii State Archives on Iolani Palace grounds holds Kingdom, Republic, and Territorial government records including vital records, land records, immigration records, court records, and the Royal collections. The Bureau of Conveyances at 1151 Punchbowl Street maintains ALL Hawaii land records in two systems: the Regular System (grantor-grantee index from 1845) and the Land Court System (Torrens title registration from 1903). The Hawaii State Department of Health in Honolulu holds vital records from 1842 -- births, deaths, and marriages from the Kingdom era onward, among the oldest civil registration in the US. Bishop Museum in Honolulu is the premier Pacific cultural institution with extensive Hawaiian genealogical materials (mo'oku'auhau), chiefly genealogies, Hawaiian language manuscripts, and photograph collections. The Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library holds records of the American Protestant mission from 1820 including journals, letters, and vital records compiled by missionaries. For Chinese, Japanese, and Filipino families, Honolulu was the primary port of arrival -- immigration station records, plantation labor contracts, and Chinese Exclusion Act case files are at NARA San Francisco and the Hawaii State Archives.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu maintains all Oahu land records in two systems: Regular System (grantor-grantee index from 1845) and Land Court System (Torrens title, 1903+); Great Mahele awards (1848) and Royal Patent grants are indexed; online search available

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu County Land Records -- Bureau of Conveyances / Land Court
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Conveyances, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: All Oahu land transactions in the statewide Bureau of Conveyances: Regular System (grantor-grantee, 1845+) and Land Court System (Torrens title registration, 1903+); Great Mahele awards for Oahu ahupua'a; Royal Patent grants; includes all Honolulu urban and rural properties
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent; continuous series from 1845 in centralized system; Great Mahele records from 1848

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu County Vital Records -- Hawaii Department of Health
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii Department of Health, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii civil registration from 1842 -- among the earliest in the US; births, deaths, and marriages for Oahu; Department of Health is the sole custodian; delayed birth certificates available for pre-registration births; marriage records from missionary era
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent from Kingdom era; Honolulu had the strongest compliance as the seat of government

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu Immigration Station and Plantation Labor Contract Records
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Archives / NARA San Francisco / Bishop Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Honolulu was the primary port of entry for Hawaii; immigration station passenger manifests; plantation labor contracts for Chinese, Japanese, Portuguese, Korean, and Filipino workers; Chinese Exclusion Act case files (NARA San Francisco); Board of Immigration records at Hawaii State Archives; passport and permit records for inter-island and foreign travel
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; Hawaii State Archives holds extensive immigration series; some NARA records are digitized on FamilySearch

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu County Census Records -- Kingdom and Federal
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Archives / NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii censuses (1866, 1878, 1890) with Honolulu as the primary urban enumeration; US federal census from 1900; Honolulu's multiethnic population makes census records especially rich; military personnel at Pearl Harbor and other bases appear in 1910-1950 censuses

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu Church and Missionary Records -- Kawaiahao, Mission Houses, Catholic Cathedral
source_profiles.repository: Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library / Kawaiahao Church / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kawaiahao Church (est. 1820) records including Hawaiian royal family events; American Protestant mission records from 1820 at Mission Houses Museum; Catholic Cathedral of Our Lady of Peace (1843) records; Buddhist Hongwanji Mission (1889) and Shinto shrine records for Japanese community; Mormon (LDS) records from 1850; extensive Chinese Christian mission records

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu Historic Newspapers -- English and Hawaiian Language
source_profiles.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa Hamilton Library / Papakilo Database / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hawaiian-language newspapers (1834-1948) are an extraordinary genealogical source -- digitized in the Papakilo Database and Ho'olaupa'i project; English-language papers include Pacific Commercial Advertiser (1856), Honolulu Star-Bulletin; obituaries, marriage notices, legal notices, ship arrivals, and community news for all ethnic groups

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu / Pearl Harbor Military Records -- WWII Era
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Hawaii War Records Depository (UH Manoa) / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pearl Harbor attack records (December 7, 1941); extensive WWII military personnel records for Oahu bases; Hawaii Territorial Guard; 100th Infantry Battalion / 442nd Regimental Combat Team (Japanese American unit) records; selective service and draft records; martial law records (1941-1944); veteran discharge records

source_profiles.source_name: Honolulu County Probate Records -- First Circuit Court
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Judiciary / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: First Circuit Court probate files for Oahu estates; Kingdom-era probate records; includes wills, inventories, administrations, guardianships, and accounts; estate records often document family relationships, property holdings, and heirs across multiple ethnic communities; Hawaiian ali'i (chiefly) estates are extensively documented
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; historical probate files transferred to Hawaii State Archives

county_metadata.parent_county: Territory of Hawaii (county system created by Act 39, Session Laws 1905)
county_metadata.record_losses: Kalawao County has no independent county government. It is administered by the Hawaii Department of Health. Records for the Kalaupapa leprosy settlement are held by the Hawaii State Archives and the Department of Health, not by a county clerk. Patient records have restricted access due to privacy laws.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kalawao County was formed in 1905 under Act 39 of the Territorial Legislature. It consists solely of the Kalaupapa peninsula on the north coast of Molokai. It is the smallest county in the United States by both area and population. The county has no functioning county government and is administered by the Hawaii Department of Health. The Kalaupapa leprosy (Hansen's disease) settlement was established in 1866 by King Kamehameha V. Over 8,000 people were sent to Kalaupapa between 1866 and 1969. Father Damien de Veuster served the community from 1873-1889. Kalaupapa National Historical Park was established in 1980 (redesignated 2009). Records are subject to privacy restrictions under Hawaii law.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kalawao County is the smallest county in the United States by area and population, consisting solely of the Kalaupapa peninsula on the north shore of Molokai. Established in 1866 as a leprosy (Hansen's disease) isolation settlement by King Kamehameha V, the settlement received patients from all Hawaiian islands until 1969. Kalawao has no functioning county government -- it is administered by the Hawaii Department of Health. Genealogical research for Kalaupapa patients requires navigating strict privacy protections under Hawaii Revised Statutes. The Board of Health records at the Hawaii State Archives document patient arrivals, deaths, and the administration of the settlement. Father Damien (Saint Damien de Veuster) served the community from 1873-1889 and his records are partially preserved at the Sacred Hearts Archives in Belgium and locally. The National Park Service (Kalaupapa National Historical Park, est. 2009) maintains historical records and oral histories. Patient burial records at Kalaupapa and Kalawao cemeteries are among the most poignant genealogical sources. Research tip: families of Kalaupapa patients should contact the Department of Health directly, as many records are restricted and require proof of kinship for access.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Kalawao County land records are in the statewide Bureau of Conveyances system in Honolulu; the Kalaupapa peninsula lands were set aside by the Board of Health in 1866; land transactions are minimal due to the settlement's unique administrative status

source_profiles.source_name: Kalaupapa Settlement Death Records and Burial Registers
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii Department of Health / Hawaii State Archives / Kalaupapa NHP
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Death records and burial registers for Kalaupapa leprosy settlement from 1866; over 8,000 patients were sent to the settlement; records include patient name, home island, date of admission, date of death, and burial location; some records have restricted access under Hawaii privacy laws
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival at Hawaii State Archives and Department of Health; some early records are fragmentary

source_profiles.source_name: Kalaupapa Settlement Board of Health Administrative Records
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Archives / Hawaii Department of Health
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Board of Health records documenting the administration of the Kalaupapa settlement: patient admission registers, correspondence, superintendent reports, inspection reports, and policy records; these administrative files often contain biographical details about patients and staff including Father Damien and Mother Marianne Cope
source_profiles.survival_notes: Substantial survival at Hawaii State Archives; some records restricted

source_profiles.source_name: Kalaupapa Church Records -- St. Philomena (Father Damien), Siloama
source_profiles.repository: Sacred Hearts Archives / Hawaii State Archives / Kalaupapa NHP
source_profiles.coverage_notes: St. Philomena Catholic Church records maintained by Father Damien (1873-1889) and successors; Siloama Protestant Church records; baptisms, marriages, and burials performed at the settlement; church records supplement official Board of Health records and sometimes provide family details not in government files

source_profiles.source_name: Kalawao County Census Records -- Kingdom and Federal
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Archives / NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii censuses enumerated Kalaupapa settlement residents separately; US federal census from 1900 lists settlement patients and staff; census records provide snapshots of the settlement population including ethnicity, age, and birthplace of patients

source_profiles.source_name: Kalaupapa Cemeteries and Memorial Records
source_profiles.repository: Kalaupapa National Historical Park / Hawaii Department of Health
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Multiple cemeteries at Kalaupapa and Kalawao including Catholic, Protestant, and Buddhist burial grounds; memorial records and grave markers; NPS has conducted burial documentation and oral history projects; cemetery records help identify patients whose administrative records are incomplete

source_profiles.source_name: Hawaiian-Language Newspaper References to Kalaupapa Patients
source_profiles.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa / Papakilo Database
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hawaiian-language newspapers published extensive coverage of the Kalaupapa settlement including lists of patients sent to Molokai, appeals from families, legislative debates, and community commentary; the Papakilo Database and nupepa.org provide searchable digitized Hawaiian newspapers

county_metadata.parent_county: Territory of Hawaii (county system created by Act 39, Session Laws 1905)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kauai County was formed in 1905 under Act 39 of the Territorial Legislature, encompassing the islands of Kauai and Niihau. County seat is Lihue. Kauai is the oldest of the main Hawaiian islands and was the last island to be unified under Kamehameha I -- King Kaumualii of Kauai ceded by treaty in 1810. Kauai's historical districts include Hanalei, Kawaihau, Lihue, Koloa, and Waimea. Niihau, included in Kauai County, has been privately owned by the Robinson family since 1864 and maintains a Native Hawaiian community. Sugar plantations dominated Kauai's economy from the 1830s through the 20th century. Hurricane Iniki devastated the island in September 1992.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kauai County includes the islands of Kauai and Niihau. Kauai is the oldest of the main Hawaiian islands and was the last to be unified under Kamehameha I (by treaty in 1810 under King Kaumualii). The Kauai Historical Society in Lihue holds local history collections, photographs, and oral histories, and publishes 'The Kauai Papers' and other genealogically relevant materials. Kauai's sugar plantation records are critical for immigrant genealogy -- Grove Farm Homestead Museum in Lihue preserves the records of one of Hawaii's most important plantations, documenting Japanese, Filipino, Portuguese, and Chinese workers from the 1860s onward. Land records are at the Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu; Great Mahele awards for Kauai can be searched in the Indices of Awards at the Hawaii State Archives. Waioli Huiia Church in Hanalei has mission-era records from the 1830s. Niihau, privately owned by the Robinson family since 1864, has a Hawaiian-speaking population whose records are limited -- the Robinson family archives are largely private. Hurricane Iniki (1992) damaged some local records and buildings on Kauai. The Kauai Museum in Lihue has exhibits and archival materials on the island's multicultural heritage. Buddhist and Shinto temple records on Kauai document Japanese community vital events.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu maintains all Kauai and Niihau land records; Regular System from 1845 and Land Court System from 1903; Great Mahele awards for Kauai ahupua'a are indexed; Niihau land records reflect Robinson family ownership

source_profiles.source_name: Kauai County Land Records -- Bureau of Conveyances
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Conveyances, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: All Kauai and Niihau land transactions recorded in the statewide Bureau of Conveyances system; Great Mahele awards for Kauai ahupua'a; Royal Patent grants; kuleana awards to native tenants; Land Court System (1903+) for registered land titles

source_profiles.source_name: Kauai County Vital Records -- Hawaii Department of Health
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii Department of Health, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii civil registration from 1842 for Kauai births, deaths, and marriages; district agents on Kauai recorded events; delayed birth certificates for earlier events; marriage records from missionary era churches on Kauai
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good from Kingdom era; some early records for remote Kauai communities may have gaps

source_profiles.source_name: Kauai County Census Records -- Kingdom and Federal
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Archives / NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii censuses for Kauai and Niihau (1866, 1878, 1890); US federal census from 1900; Kauai plantation communities enumerated in detail; Niihau's Native Hawaiian population appears in census records; agricultural schedules document sugar operations

source_profiles.source_name: Kauai Sugar Plantation Employment and Labor Records
source_profiles.repository: Grove Farm Homestead Museum / University of Hawaii at Manoa / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kauai plantation records including Grove Farm (est. 1864), Koloa Plantation (est. 1835, Hawaii's first sugar plantation), Lihue Plantation, and McBryde Sugar; labor contracts and employment records for Japanese, Filipino, Portuguese, Chinese, and Korean workers; payroll records, housing records, and plantation store accounts
source_profiles.survival_notes: Grove Farm records are exceptionally well preserved; other plantation records vary

source_profiles.source_name: Kauai Church Records -- Waioli Mission, Koloa Church, Buddhist Temples
source_profiles.repository: Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library / Local congregations / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Waioli Huiia Church, Hanalei (est. 1834) mission records; Koloa Church records from the 1830s (one of the earliest mission stations); Catholic parish records; Buddhist temple records for Japanese communities in Lihue, Koloa, and Waimea; Hawaiian Evangelical Association records for Kauai congregations

source_profiles.source_name: Kauai County Probate and Circuit Court Records -- Fifth Circuit
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Judiciary / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Fifth Circuit Court probate and civil records for Kauai and Niihau; Kingdom-era court records; estates, guardianships, land disputes; naturalization petitions; divorce records; adoption records (restricted)
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; historical records at Hawaii State Archives

source_profiles.source_name: Kauai Historic Newspapers -- The Garden Island
source_profiles.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa / Kauai Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Garden Island newspaper (est. 1902) is the primary Kauai newspaper with obituaries, marriage notices, legal notices, plantation news, and community events; Hawaiian-language newspapers also covered Kauai extensively; digitized through Chronicling America and UH Manoa projects

county_metadata.parent_county: Territory of Hawaii (county system created by Act 39, Session Laws 1905)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Maui County was formed in 1905 under Act 39 of the Territorial Legislature, encompassing the islands of Maui, Molokai (except the Kalawao peninsula), Lanai, and the uninhabited island of Kahoolawe. County seat is Wailuku. Lahaina on west Maui was the capital of the Kingdom of Hawaii from 1820-1845 and a major Pacific whaling port. Maui's historical districts include Lahaina, Wailuku, Makawao, Hana, and Kipahulu. Lanai was a Dole pineapple plantation from 1922-1992. Molokai (non-Kalawao portions) includes the historical communities of Kaunakakai, Halawa, and Kualapuu. The August 2023 Lahaina wildfire caused catastrophic damage to the historic whaling town and surrounding communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Maui County encompasses the islands of Maui, Molokai (except the Kalawao peninsula), Lanai, and Kahoolawe. The county seat is Wailuku, where the Maui Historical Society and Bailey House Museum hold local records, missionary-era documents, and Hawaiian artifacts. Lahaina on west Maui was the capital of the Kingdom of Hawaii from 1820-1845 and a major whaling port -- Lahaina's historical records include ship logs, whaler records, and early missionary accounts from Lahainaluna Seminary (est. 1831, the oldest school west of the Rockies). The devastating Lahaina wildfire of August 2023 destroyed significant portions of the historic town; researchers should verify the status of any local records or repositories. Sugar and pineapple plantation records for Maui (Hawaiian Commercial & Sugar, Maui Pineapple Company) document immigrant workers from the 1870s onward. Land records are at the Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu; Great Mahele awards for Maui are indexed at the Hawaii State Archives. Molokai's records (outside Kalawao) fall under Maui County -- the Molokai Museum holds local community records. Lanai was largely a Dole pineapple plantation from 1922-1992 -- plantation employment records are genealogically significant for Filipino families. Ka'ahumanu Church in Wailuku (est. 1832) has early mission-era records for central Maui congregants.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu maintains all Maui, Molokai, Lanai, and Kahoolawe land records; Regular System from 1845 and Land Court System from 1903; Great Mahele awards for Maui districts are indexed; Lahaina-era records from the Kingdom capital

source_profiles.source_name: Maui County Land Records -- Bureau of Conveyances
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Conveyances, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: All Maui, Molokai (non-Kalawao), Lanai, and Kahoolawe land transactions recorded in the statewide Bureau of Conveyances system; Great Mahele awards for Maui and Molokai ahupua'a; Royal Patent grants; Lahaina-era land records from when it was the Kingdom capital

source_profiles.source_name: Maui County Vital Records -- Hawaii Department of Health
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii Department of Health, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii civil registration from 1842 for Maui, Molokai, and Lanai; Lahaina records are among the earliest as the former capital; births, deaths, marriages registered through district agents; delayed birth certificates available
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good from Kingdom era; Lahaina-area records are especially early due to the capital's presence there

source_profiles.source_name: Maui County Census Records -- Kingdom and Federal
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Archives / NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kingdom of Hawaii censuses for Maui, Molokai, and Lanai (1866, 1878, 1890); US federal census from 1900; plantation communities on Maui and Lanai enumerated in detail; Molokai population includes both Hawaiian and immigrant communities

source_profiles.source_name: Maui Sugar and Pineapple Plantation Employment Records
source_profiles.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa Hamilton Library / Maui Historical Society / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hawaiian Commercial & Sugar Company (HC&S) records for central Maui; Maui Pineapple Company records; Dole plantation records for Lanai (1922-1992) documenting primarily Filipino workers; sugar plantation records for Lahaina, Wailuku, Paia, and Puunene; labor contracts, payroll, and housing records for multiethnic workforce
source_profiles.survival_notes: Varies by plantation; some extensive collections at UH Manoa

source_profiles.source_name: Maui County Church Records -- Lahainaluna, Ka'ahumanu, Wailuku Mission
source_profiles.repository: Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library / Maui Historical Society / Local congregations
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lahainaluna Seminary (est. 1831) records from the missionary era; Ka'ahumanu Church, Wailuku (est. 1832) records; Wainee/Waiola Church, Lahaina (est. 1823) records including Hawaiian royal family events; Catholic, Buddhist, and Shinto records for Maui's multiethnic communities; Molokai and Lanai church records

source_profiles.source_name: Maui County Probate and Circuit Court Records -- Second Circuit
source_profiles.repository: Hawaii State Judiciary / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Second Circuit Court probate and civil records for Maui, Molokai (non-Kalawao), and Lanai; Kingdom-era court records from Lahaina (former capital had an active court); estates, land disputes, guardianships, naturalization petitions, and divorce records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival; Kingdom-era Lahaina court records are historically significant

source_profiles.source_name: Lahaina Whaling Port Records and Ship Logs
source_profiles.repository: Maui Historical Society / New Bedford Whaling Museum / Hawaii State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lahaina was one of the Pacific's premier whaling ports from the 1820s-1860s; ship logs, crew lists, port records, and customs house records document sailors from America, Europe, and the Pacific; some whalers settled in Hawaii and married into Hawaiian families; records at Maui Historical Society and whaling museums
source_profiles.survival_notes: Fragmentary; many records dispersed to whaling museums in New England

source_profiles.source_name: Maui County Cemetery Records -- Maui, Molokai, Lanai
source_profiles.repository: Maui County / Find A Grave / Maui Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery records for Wailuku, Lahaina, Makawao, Hana, and Kihei cemeteries on Maui; Molokai community cemeteries; Lanai plantation-era cemeteries; burial permits from the Board of Health; Hawaiian church and family burial sites; Japanese, Chinese, and Filipino community cemeteries
